title: R Package
text: The statsdata R package gives you direct access to the StatsData Hub collection from within R — search for datasets by teaching criteria and load them straight into your analysis without leaving RStudio.

title: R Package
text:
Installation
The package is currently available from GitHub. To install it, you first need the devtools package:
install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("emilysomerset/statsdata-hub/statsdata")

title: R Package
text:
Basic usage

Search for datasets
Find datasets by statistical method, teaching concept, course level, or discipline:
library(statsdata)

# Browse all available datasets
find_datasets()

# Find datasets suitable for teaching logistic regression
find_datasets(method = "logistic_regression")

# Find datasets for a graduate course in psychology
find_datasets(course_level = "graduate", discipline = "psychology")


Search by specific criteria
# Search by statistical method
search_by_method("multilevel_modeling")

# Search by teaching concept
search_by_concept("confounding")

# Search by variable type
search_by_variable("count")


Load a dataset
# Load a dataset by its ID
titanic <- load_dataset("titanic-survival")
head(titanic)

# Load and start analysing immediately
library(statsdata)
busy <- load_dataset("busy-wellbeing")


Get information about a dataset
Each dataset has a full metadata entry — you can see the available datasets and their IDs using find_datasets(), then load the one you want with load_dataset().

title: R Package
text:
Available datasets
See the Browse Datasets page for the full filterable collection.

title: About
text: About this site

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text: Data from a field experiment (N=774) testing whether rewarding gym attendance individually versus as a pair (“tandem”) changes behavior. In the standard condition, participants earned a cash reward for each day they visited the gym. In the tandem condition, participants earned the same reward only if they visited the gym together with an assigned partner. Despite this extra hurdle, tandem-reward participants visited the gym about 35% more frequently than those in the standard condition. Data are in long format with weekly gym visit counts across a 10-week pre-intervention baseline, 4-week intervention period, and 2-week post-intervention follow-up. Ideal for teaching analysis of randomized experiments, repeated measures, and pre/post intervention designs.

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Gershon R, Cryder C, Milkman K. Friends with health benefits.
URL: https://researchbox.org/2266
License: CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text:
Dataset Characteristics

Observations: 12384
Variables: 12
Missingness present: FALSE
Longitudinal: TRUE
Hierarchical: TRUE

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text:
Variable Dictionary








Variable
Type
Description




ppid
categorical
Unique participant identifier


pair
categorical
Identifier linking paired participants in the tandem condition


condition
categorical
Assigned condition: standard (individual reward) or tandem (reward requires visiting with a partner)


week_char
categorical
Original week label (e.g. pre10, pre1, 1-4, post1, post2)


week_visits
count
Number of gym visits in that week


any_visit
binary
Indicator for whether the participant visited the gym at all that week


week_num
ordinal
Standardized week number: negative values are pre-intervention weeks (-10 to -1), 1-4 are intervention weeks, 5-6 are post-intervention weeks


pre_intervention
binary
Indicator for the pre-intervention baseline period (10 weeks)


intervention
binary
Indicator for the 4-week intervention period


post_intervention
binary
Indicator for the 2-week post-intervention follow-up period


Gender
binary
Participant gender


medianexer
categorical
Whether the participant’s pre-intervention average gym usage was above/equal to or below the sample median

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Analyze a randomized field experiment with a pre/during/post design
Compare gym visit frequency between treatment conditions using a t-test or regression
Account for repeated weekly observations within the same participant
Use baseline (pre-intervention) behavior as a covariate to improve precision

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Compare average weekly visits between conditions during the intervention period only
Plot weekly visit rates over time by condition to visualize the intervention effect
Test whether the treatment effect differs by baseline exercise level (medianexer)

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Ignoring that participants are paired (the ‘pair’ variable) within the tandem condition, which creates dependence between observations
Comparing post-intervention averages without accounting for differences in baseline (pre-intervention) gym usage

title: Friends with Health Benefits: Tandem vs. Individual Gym Incentives
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("gym-incentives")

title: Social Pressure to be Busy and Emotional Wellbeing
text: A daily diary study examining how social pressure to appear busy relates to emotional wellbeing. Data were collected from 261 participants over approximately 7 days each, yielding 1,827 person-day observations. Participants reported daily busyness, positive affect, negative affect, anxiety, and depressive symptoms, alongside person-level demographics, socioeconomic status, Big Five personality traits, and emotional complexity measures. Ideal for teaching multilevel modeling, within-person centering, and longitudinal data analysis.

title: Social Pressure to be Busy and Emotional Wellbeing
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Lua VYQ, Majeed NM, Leung AK, Hartanto A. (2022). A daily within-person investigation on the link between social expectancies to be busy and emotional wellbeing: the moderating role of emotional complexity acceptance. Cognition and Emotion, 1-8. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2022.2054778
URL: https://researchbox.org/234
License: CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

title: Social Pressure to be Busy and Emotional Wellbeing
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Explain the difference between between-person and within-person effects
Apply group mean centering to separate within- and between-person variance
Fit a multilevel model with person-level and day-level predictors
Interpret fixed and random effects in a longitudinal model

title: Social Pressure to be Busy and Emotional Wellbeing
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Compare a naive OLS regression ignoring clustering to a multilevel model, and discuss what changes
Visualize within-person trajectories of busyness and affect across days

title: Social Pressure to be Busy and Emotional Wellbeing
text: Common Student Misconceptions

title: Social Pressure to be Busy and Emotional Wellbeing
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("busy-wellbeing")

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text: Data from Experiment 2 of a study examining how subtle shifts in language perspective shape children’s social judgments. A total of 100 children aged 6-9 years completed 8 trials in which a child speaker described classroom norms using either generic pronouns (we/you) or specific pronouns (referencing a named individual). Children judged whether the speaker was generous after each trial. The design is within-subjects for language type (generic vs. specific) and between-subjects for pronoun type (we vs. generic you), with counterbalancing via two presentation orders (A and B). Results showed children were more likely to judge speakers as generous when using generic pronouns, with stronger effects for we than generic you. Ideal for teaching multilevel logistic regression, mixed within- and between-subjects designs, and binary outcome analysis with repeated measures.

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Orvell A, Simmons E, Umscheid V, Elli G, Gelman SA. From ‘me’ to ‘we’: How perspective shifts in language can shape children’s judgments about kindness, caring, and inclusivity. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General.
URL: https://researchbox.org/3371
License: CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text:
Dataset Characteristics

Observations: 800
Variables: 11
Missingness present: FALSE
Longitudinal: FALSE
Hierarchical: TRUE

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text:
Variable Dictionary








Variable
Type
Description




ID
categorical
Unique participant identifier


age
continuous
Participant exact age in years (range approximately 6-9)


age_c
continuous
Age mean-centered (age minus sample mean age)


age_group
ordinal
Age group in months (e.g., 67 = 67 months)


Generic
categorical
Between-subjects pronoun condition: We (generic we) or You (generic you)


GenOrder
categorical
Counterbalancing order: A (generic pronoun in Trials 1-4, specific in 5-8) or B (specific in Trials 1-4, generic in 5-8)


Attribution
ordinal
Attribution measure collected at end of session


gender
categorical
Participant gender (F = female, M = male)


Trial
ordinal
Trial number within the session (Trial 1 through Trial 8)


Answer
binary
Child’s generosity judgment for the speaker on this trial (1 = generous, 0 = not generous)


Language
categorical
Language type used by the speaker on this trial: Generic (we or you pronoun) or Specific (named individual); derived from Trial number and GenOrder

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Fit a multilevel logistic regression model with a binary outcome and repeated observations nested within children
Distinguish between within-subjects (language type) and between-subjects (pronoun type) factors
Interpret fixed effects for language condition on the log-odds of a generosity judgment
Explain why ignoring clustering by participant inflates Type I error in repeated-measures designs

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Compare the proportion of generous judgments for generic vs. specific language using a simple proportion test, then discuss why a multilevel model is more appropriate
Examine whether the generic language effect differs by pronoun type (we vs. you) using an interaction term
Plot individual-level response profiles across 8 trials to visualize within-person variability

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Treating each of the 8 trial-level observations from the same child as independent
Confusing the within-subjects manipulation (generic vs. specific language per trial) with the between-subjects factor (we vs. you condition)

title: From Me to We: Generic Pronouns and Children’s Judgments of Generosity (Experiment 2)
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("me-to-we")

title: Titanic Passenger Survival

title: Contribute a Dataset
text: StatsData Hub is an open community resource — contributions of new datasets are welcome and encouraged. Each dataset needs two things: a data file and a metadata description. Here’s how to add one.

title: Contribute a Dataset
section: What makes a good dataset for this repository?
text:
What makes a good dataset for this repository?
A good teaching dataset should:

Illustrate one or more specific statistical methods clearly
Have a meaningful real-world context students can engage with
Be openly licensed (CC0, CC BY, or similar) so others can freely use it
Be appropriate for classroom use (no sensitive personal data)
Come with a citable source (published study, public repository, or well-known teaching dataset)

title: Contribute a Dataset
text:
Step-by-step contribution guide

1. Fork the repository
Go to github.com/emilysomerset/statsdata-hub and click Fork (top right). This creates your own copy of the project to work in.


2. Add the data file
Place your cleaned dataset as a CSV file at:
data/clean//.csv
For example:
data/clean/titanic-survival/titanic-survival.csv
If the data required cleaning or processing, add your processing script at:
scripts/process-.R


3. Create a metadata YAML file
Copy the template below and save it as:
metadata/datasets/.yaml
Fill in every field — the more complete the metadata, the more useful the dataset is for instructors searching for specific teaching needs.
id: your-dataset-id
title: "Your Dataset Title"
description: >
  A clear description of what the dataset contains, where it came from,
  and what it is commonly used to teach.

source:
  citation: "Author(s) (Year). Title. Journal/Source."
  url: "https://link-to-source.com"
  license: "CC0-1.0"

file: "data/clean/your-dataset-id/your-dataset-id.csv"

data_source_type: published_study  # or: classic_dataset, public_repository, simulated

methods: [logistic_regression]     # see full list below
concepts: [confounding]            # see full list below
variable_types: [binary, continuous]
course_level: [introductory]       # introductory, intermediate, advanced_undergraduate, graduate
discipline: [health]               # see full list below

characteristics:
  n_obs: 1000
  n_vars: 10
  missingness: false
  survey_weights: false
  longitudinal: false
  hierarchical: false
  cross_sectional: true

teaching:
  learning_objectives:
    - "Learning objective 1"
    - "Learning objective 2"
  classroom_activities:
    - "Suggested activity 1"
  common_misconceptions:
    - "Common misconception 1"

variables:
  - name: column_name
    type: continuous
    description: "What this variable measures"


4. Open a pull request
Once you’ve added the data file and YAML, open a pull request on GitHub. Describe what the dataset is and why it would be useful for teaching. A maintainer will review it and let you know if anything needs updating.

title: Contribute a Dataset
text:
Valid metadata values
Statistical methods: t_test, anova, linear_regression, logistic_regression, poisson_regression, survival_analysis, pca, clustering, survey_sampling, bayesian_methods, time_series, multilevel_modeling, factor_analysis, mixed_effects
Teaching concepts: confounding, simpsons_paradox, missing_data, outliers, heteroskedasticity, multicollinearity, nonlinearity, selection_bias, clustered_observations, measurement_error, overdispersion
Disciplines: health, biology, psychology, economics, sports, climate, education, survey_research, finance, politics

title: Contribute a Dataset
text:
Questions?
Open an issue on GitHub and I’ll be happy to help.

title: Virtual Reality vs. Video vs. Paper Business Case Studies: Learning Outcomes
text: Data from a randomized experiment (N=148) comparing learning outcomes across three business case study formats: paper-based, video-based, and virtual reality (VR). Participants were randomly assigned to one condition and completed a business case study, then responded to measures of presence (IPQ), motivation (IMMS), task load (TLI), technology adoption intentions, and a knowledge recall quiz. VR participants demonstrated superior recall of both factual and visual details compared to paper and video conditions. Missingness in adoption items is structural: participants only rated adoption intentions for their assigned medium. Ideal for teaching one-way ANOVA, planned contrasts, structural missing data, and analysis of experimental between-subjects designs with multiple outcome measures.

title: Virtual Reality vs. Video vs. Paper Business Case Studies: Learning Outcomes
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Hubbard TD. (2025). Transforming business education: the impact of virtual reality on learning outcomes in case studies. Frontiers in Virtual Reality, 6. https://doi.org/10.3389/frvir.2025.1461690
URL: https://researchbox.org/3216
License: CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

title: Virtual Reality vs. Video vs. Paper Business Case Studies: Learning Outcomes
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Conduct a one-way ANOVA comparing three experimental conditions
Use planned contrasts to test specific hypotheses about VR vs. paper and video
Distinguish between structural missingness (by design) and problematic missingness
Interpret multiple dependent variables from a single experiment

title: Virtual Reality vs. Video vs. Paper Business Case Studies: Learning Outcomes
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Compare mean knowledge recall scores across conditions and test for significant differences
Discuss why adoption items are missing for non-assigned conditions and whether this is a problem
Explore whether presence (IPQ) mediates the effect of condition on learning outcomes

title: Virtual Reality vs. Video vs. Paper Business Case Studies: Learning Outcomes
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Treating structural NAs in condition-specific adoption items as data quality problems
Ignoring multiple comparisons when testing all pairwise differences between three conditions

title: Virtual Reality vs. Video vs. Paper Business Case Studies: Learning Outcomes
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("vr-business-education")

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text: Longitudinal data from a clinical trial of the anti-epileptic drug progabide. Each of 59 patients was observed over 4 follow-up periods, with seizure counts recorded at each visit, alongside baseline seizure count and age. Commonly used to teach Poisson regression for count data with repeated measurements on the same subject.

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Thall and Vail (1990), distributed in the MASS R package
URL: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/MASS/
License: Available via the MASS package (part of base R distribution)

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:

Variables: 9
Missingness present: FALSE
Longitudinal: TRUE
Hierarchical: TRUE

Patient identifier (1-59), repeated across 4 rows each

continuous

Log-transformed, centered age

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Fit a Poisson regression model for count outcomes
Recognize the need to account for repeated measurements per subject
Compare naive Poisson models to overdispersion-corrected alternatives

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Model seizure counts as a function of treatment and baseline rate, ignoring then accounting for repeated measures

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Treating each of the 4 visits per patient as an independent observation

title: Epilepsy Seizure Counts (Progabide Trial)
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("epilepsy-seizures")

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text: A dataset of 3,915 Yelp reviews comparing consumer sentiment toward plant-based fast food menu items (e.g., “Impossible,” “Beyond,” “Sofritas”) versus traditional meat-based controls (e.g., “Whopper,” “Carnitas”) within the same restaurant chains, spanning 7 fast-food chains and hundreds of unique locations. Reviews were scored using transformer-based sentiment analysis (RoBERTa) for sentiment valence, and a large language model extraction procedure flagged mentions of sensory attributes, texture, and environmental/ethical motivations. Using fixed-effects and linear mixed models to control for restaurant, user, and temporal heterogeneity, plant-based items were found to receive significantly higher star ratings (β=0.73) and more positive sentiment valence (β=0.33) than meat-based controls, an effect driven primarily by moral utility rather than superior sensory performance. Ideal for teaching mixed-effects models, text/sentiment data, and analysis of nested/clustered review data (reviews nested within users and within restaurant locations).

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Healey, P.M. (2026). Plant-based vs. meat-based: a comparative sentiment analysis of consumer reviews in fast food settings. Food Quality and Preference, 144, 105995. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodqual.2026.105995
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0950329326001497
License: See publisher for reuse terms

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text:
Dataset Characteristics

Observations: 3915
Variables: 24
Missingness present: FALSE
Longitudinal: TRUE
Hierarchical: TRUE

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text:
Variable Dictionary








Variable
Type
Description




review_id
categorical
A unique alphanumeric identifier assigned by Yelp to each individual review observation


user_id
categorical
A unique identifier for the Yelp user, used as a random intercept in mixed-effects models to control for individual reviewer stringency or bias


business_id
categorical
A unique identifier for the specific restaurant location, used to cluster standard errors and as a random intercept to account for store-level quality


chain_key
categorical
A standardized, case-insensitive categorical variable used as a fixed effect to represent the parent restaurant franchise


chain_name
categorical
The descriptive name of the restaurant chain (e.g., Burger King, Chipotle)


city
categorical
The geographic city of the restaurant location, used as a high-dimensional fixed effect to control for local market heterogeneity


state
spatial
The US state where the restaurant business is registered in the Yelp metadata


stars
ordinal
The primary dependent variable: consumer’s ordinal rating of the experience on a 1-to-5 scale


date
date_time
Timestamp for the review (study window: January 1, 2010 to December 31, 2021)


text
text
The raw prose of the review used for hierarchical keyword classification and transformer-based sentiment inference


useful
count
Count of other users who voted the review as ‘useful’


funny
count
Count of other users who voted the review as ‘funny’


cool
count
Count of other users who voted the review as ‘cool’


item_label
categorical
Categorical assignment identifying whether a review belongs to the plant-based ‘target’ group or the meat-based ‘control’ group


target_canonical
categorical
The specific plant-based term (e.g., ‘impossible,’ ‘sofritas’) detected in the text that triggered the target classification


control_canonical
categorical
The specific meat-based term (e.g., ‘chicken,’ ‘steak’) detected when no target keywords were present


sent_label
categorical
Discrete categorical sentiment output (Positive, Negative, or Neutral) from the RoBERTa sentiment classification model


sent_score
continuous
Probability or confidence level associated with the sentiment label, ranging from 0 to 1


sent_valence
continuous
Continuous sentiment metric derived from the label and score, ranging from -1.0 to +1.0; the primary textual dependent variable


sensory_mention
binary
Binary flag indicating presence of text related to physical food attributes (e.g., taste, flavor)


texture_specific
binary
Binary flag identifying reviews that specifically discuss texture or mouthfeel (e.g., crispy, rubbery)


environmental_mention
binary
Binary flag indicating mentions of sustainability, animal welfare, or ethical motivations; used to test the moral utility hypothesis


target
binary
Binary numeric representation of item_label (1 = Target/plant-based, 0 = Control/meat-based), used as the primary independent variable


month
date_time
Month and year of the review, used to control for seasonal shocks and longitudinal trends

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Fit a mixed-effects model with crossed random intercepts for user and restaurant location
Use fixed effects to control for chain, geography, and time
Work with text-derived sentiment scores as a continuous outcome variable
Test a mediation-style hypothesis: does an environmental mention explain the rating difference between plant-based and meat-based items?

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Compare a naive regression of stars on item_label to a mixed-effects model accounting for user and restaurant clustering
Explore whether the plant-based rating advantage holds across different chains
Use environmental_mention and sensory_mention as predictors to test competing explanations for the rating gap

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Treating reviews from the same user or restaurant as independent observations
Assuming sentiment_score and sentiment_valence measure the same thing without checking how they are constructed
Interpreting a positive association between plant-based items and ratings as evidence of superior taste, without considering the environmental halo explanation

title: Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("plantbased-meat-reviews")

title: Everyday Discrimination and Cognitive Failures (Singapore Daily Diary Study)
text: A seven-day daily diary study examining the association between everyday discrimination and cognitive failures among 484 undergraduate students at Singapore universities (Study 1 from Majeed et al., 2023). Participants completed nightly questionnaires reporting daily discrimination experiences, cognitive failures, and daily stressor exposure, alongside person-level demographics and socioeconomic variables. Multilevel modelling revealed that experiencing discrimination was associated with poorer cognitive functioning at both within- and between-person levels, even after controlling for demographic covariates and daily stressor exposure. Ideal for teaching multilevel modelling, within-person centering, and the study of discrimination and cognitive health in a daily diary context.

title: Everyday Discrimination and Cognitive Failures (Singapore Daily Diary Study)
text:
Source and Citation

Citation: Majeed NM, Kasturiratna KS, Lua VY, Li MY, Hartanto A. (2023). Discrimination and cognitive failures in Singapore and the US: An investigation of between- and within-persons associations through multilevel modelling. International Journal of Intercultural Relations, 94, 101805. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijintrel.2023.101805
URL: https://researchbox.org/450
License: CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Gender (1 = female, 0 = male)


female.f
categorical
Gender as factor (YES = female, NO = male)


nonChinese
binary
Ethnicity (0 = Chinese, 1 = non-Chinese)


nonChinese.f
categorical
Ethnicity as factor (YES = non-Chinese, NO = Chinese)


sesInc
ordinal
Household income bracket (1-6; higher values reflect higher household income)


sesInc.c
continuous
Grand mean centred household income


sesLad
ordinal
Subjective socioeconomic status in community (1 = lowest, 10 = highest)


sesLad.c
continuous
Grand mean centred community ladder


discrimination
continuous
Daily discrimination composite score (mean of 9 items)


itemDisc1
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): You were treated with less courtesy or respect than other people


itemDisc2
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): You received poorer service than other people at restaurants or stores


itemDisc3
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): People acted as if they think you are not smart


itemDisc4
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): People acted as if they are afraid of you


itemDisc5
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): People acted as if they think you are dishonest


itemDisc6
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): People acted as if they are better than you


itemDisc7
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): You were called names or insulted


itemDisc8
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): You were threatened or harassed


itemDisc9
ordinal
[ITEM] Discrimination (1=never, 4=often): You were followed around in stores


dayCogFail
continuous
Daily cognitive failures composite score (mean of 13 items)


dayCogFail.m
continuous
Person mean of dayCogFail (between-person component)


itemDayCogFail01
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Did not notice signposts when driving/walking


itemDayCogFail02
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Forgot the purpose of going somewhere


itemDayCogFail03
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Failed to notice something pointed out to you


itemDayCogFail04
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Accidentally threw away something you meant to keep


itemDayCogFail05
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Daydreamed when you should have been concentrating


itemDayCogFail06
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Forgot whether you had done something


itemDayCogFail07
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Failed to read information properly before proceeding


itemDayCogFail08
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Said something and later wondered if it made sense


itemDayCogFail09
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Failed to take notice of something on first reading


itemDayCogFail10
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Lost track of what you were saying mid-sentence


itemDayCogFail11
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Forgot an important appointment


itemDayCogFail12
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Had trouble concentrating on a task


itemDayCogFail13
count
[ITEM] Cognitive failure (0=never, 3=many times): Forgot where you had put something


dayStressorExp
binary
Daily stressor exposure (1 = experienced at least one stressor, 0 = none)


dayStressorExp.m
continuous
Person mean of dayStressorExp (between-person component)


dayStressorExp.f
categorical
Daily stressor exposure as factor (YES/NO)


itemDayStressorArgue
binary
[ITEM] Stressor (0=no, 1=yes): Had an argument or disagreement with someone


itemDayStressorAvoid
binary
[ITEM] Stressor (0=no, 1=yes): Avoided someone to prevent an argument


itemDayStressorWork
binary
[ITEM] Stressor (0=no, 1=yes): Had a work- or school-related stressor


itemDayStressorHome
binary
[ITEM] Stressor (0=no, 1=yes): Had a home- or family-related stressor


itemDayStressorDiscrimination
binary
[ITEM] Stressor (0=no, 1=yes): Experienced discrimination or prejudice


itemDayStressorNetwork
binary
[ITEM] Stressor (0=no, 1=yes): Had a problem with a friend or member of your social network


itemDayStressorOther
binary
[ITEM] Stressor (0=no, 1=yes): Experienced another stressor not listed above

title: Everyday Discrimination and Cognitive Failures (Singapore Daily Diary Study)
text:
Suggested Learning Objectives

Fit a multilevel model with both within- and between-person predictors
Interpret fixed effects for discrimination on cognitive failures at the within- and between-person levels
Explain the purpose of group mean centering in daily diary designs
Control for confounds such as daily stressor exposure in a multilevel framework

title: Everyday Discrimination and Cognitive Failures (Singapore Daily Diary Study)
text:
Suggested Classroom Activities

Compare a naive OLS regression ignoring clustering to a multilevel model and discuss what changes in the discrimination coefficient
Visualize the within-person relationship between daily discrimination and daily cognitive failures across participants

title: Everyday Discrimination and Cognitive Failures (Singapore Daily Diary Study)
text:
Common Student Misconceptions

Treating repeated daily observations from the same person as independent
Confusing the within-person effect of discrimination (day-to-day fluctuations) with the between-person effect (average level across the study)

title: Everyday Discrimination and Cognitive Failures (Singapore Daily Diary Study)
text:
Load This Dataset in R
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("discrimination-cognitive")

title: StatsData Hub
text:
Most dataset repositories are built for finding data. StatsData Hub is built for teaching with data — helping instructors, TAs, and students find datasets aligned with specific statistical methods, teaching concepts, and course levels.



Browse datasets by statistical method, teaching concept, variable type, course level, and discipline
View detailed dataset pages with variable dictionaries, suggested learning objectives, classroom activities, and example analyses
